---
title: "We made a map of the doom debate. Here's where everyone actually stands."
date: "June 2026"
author:
  - Sean Herrington
  - Khai Tran
  - David Bravo
  - Kevin Caldwell
  - Paul Hindoian
  - Josh Tuffy
  - Mikaela Cankosyan
  - Christopher A. Davis
  - Maryam Hampaei
---

*This is the third post in a series titled "We made a map of the doom debate," produced as part of the AI Safety Camp 2026 "Assumptions of the Doom Debate" project, led by Sean Herrington. The [first post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/dZZD5Lviid2mALAZL/we-made-a-map-of-the-doom-debate) introduced the tree; the [second](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/AZrMGwBPMJmAcgqX8/we-made-a-map-of-the-doom-debate-here-s-how-the-breakdown) walked its nodes. This post places the people. The interactive tree is [here](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html).*

TL;DR:

- The AI-risk debate tangles two kinds of question: empirical ones, which evidence can settle, and framing choices, which it never will. Most "disagreements about how dangerous AI is" are really the second kind in disguise.
- We place the recurring figures (Yudkowsky, Bengio, LeCun, Christiano, Critch, and others) on the tree from the first two posts, by where their reasoning actually lives.
- Laid out this way, the sharp disagreements turn out narrow, often a single crux, and the apparent alliances thin, often nothing but a shared conclusion.

::: {.callout-note .meta-note collapse="true" title="Editor note (v2, tree-organized)"}
Working version of Post 3, themed to match Posts 1–2. Intro merged to one section; the gallery walks the tree top-down (Non-AI, then AI-Driven and its branches); the cruxes moved to an appendix. The gallery is the focus. Kept alongside the flat-list draft until we settle.
:::

---

## Why this map exists

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** One long intro — thesis, the inheritance from Posts 1–2, and the single reading rule (sort by mechanism), ending on the payoff. Absorbs the old gallery-lead. Emphasis stays sparing.
:::

The AI debate is confused, and people argue past each other. Two researchers can report the same probability of catastrophe and hold opposite pictures of the world; two who sit forty points apart can hold nearly the same one. A decade of shared evidence has moved almost no one toward almost anyone, and when evidence reliably fails to move a disagreement, the disagreement was probably never about evidence.

Two kinds of question are tangled in every estimate, and they behave nothing alike. Some are empirical. Does power-seeking come with capability? Would a failure be visible before it was terminal? Evidence speaks to these, and given time it settles them. The others are not empirical at all. Does an AI that quietly succeeds us count as catastrophe, or as worthy heirs? Over what horizon are we even asking? Would a well-behaved model lower your fear, or is that exactly what a dangerous one would show you? No experiment settles those, because they decide what the experiments mean. Mistake one kind of question for the other, and you can argue forever.

The first two posts in this series built a tree to hold the empirical questions straight, breaking doom into the branches that carry the decision weight: does AI raise the danger at all, through one dominant system or many, does that system know what it is doing, does it expect the catastrophe it causes. A tree like that sorts scenarios. It does not sort people, because what divides people is mostly what the tree leaves off it: the framing questions above, a handful of factual cruxes (is oversight tractable, how fast does capability arrive, can rival actors hold a line), and, loudest of all, what they would do about it. The tree leaves these off on purpose, so that everyone, whatever they believe, can still agree on its shape.

This post puts people on the tree anyway, by one rule: sort by mechanism. We walk the tree top-down and place each recurring figure on the branch whose scenario they actually expect. A branch is an agreement, not a bucket; to share one is to agree on how the catastrophe works. So when two people sit on the same branch and still read as opposites, the split is never the mechanism. It is one of three things, and we say which: how much weight they put there, what they would do about it, or what they would even count as doom. Most of what looks like a thinker standing in two places dissolves under this rule. People are famous for what they would do, or for a vivid scenario, or for insisting on a different definition of doom, rarely for a clean claim about mechanism; read one of those as a mechanism claim and they seem to span the tree, separate the question and they snap to a branch. Genuine split belief, real probability on two incompatible mechanisms, turns out to be rare, and we flag it where it is real.

One last thing to carry in. Most worldviews are located: they pick a branch and say the danger is here. A few are distributional: they do not pick a branch so much as bet the dangerous ones are nearly empty, and they attach to the branch they most deny. Walk the tree this way and the debate's real shape stops matching its volume: the sharpest opponents turn out to be a single fork apart, and the firmest-looking alliances turn out to share a conclusion and nothing else.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/e-distributional.qmd >}}

## II. AI-Driven Pathway

The worlds where AI raises the danger. Post 2 noted this covers two kinds at once: entirely new catastrophes (enfeeblement under a superintelligence) and old risks that AI sharpens (engineered bioweapons). On the tree it takes two shapes, depending on whether the danger runs through many systems at once or through a single dominant one.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/b-distributed.qmd >}}

### II.2 Single dominant AI pathway

One system carries most of the danger. Post 2's cast for it: the system frozen too late, the deceptive superintelligence, the rogue security AI that neutralizes its rivals, doing harm directly or by putting the means in one actor's hands. It splits by whether that system has an internal model of what it is doing, and, if it does, whether it expects the catastrophe it causes.

#### II.2.a Internal model pathway

The system knows what it is doing. In Post 2's terms it meets the conditions for an internal model: it understands the consequences of its actions, understands what an existential catastrophe is, and can tell when the two overlap. Keep Post 2's twist in view, because it is easy to lose: a system here need not be hostile. It can be trying to help and still take a decision that carries, say, a five percent chance of catastrophe, judging the trade worth it. What splits the branch is the fourth condition, whether the system expects its own action to raise the risk.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/a-knowing-optimizer.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/c-the-mistake.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/d-unwitting.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/adjacencies.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/conclusion.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/cuts.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/sources.qmd >}}
